topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink swam on the parchment, a brutal, undeniable indictment of my efforts. A C- in Advanced Calculus. It felt like a physical blow, stealing the air from my lungs. I stared at the grade, my hand trembling so violently I had to grip the edge of the desk to steady myself. Every formula, every late night spent wrestling with complex proofs, seemed to dissolve into meaningless scribbles. A tightness constricted my chest, a cold, heavy weight settling just beneath my ribs. I slowly pushed the paper away, the sound of the movement unnervingly loud in the quiet room. I walked to the window, the city lights blurring into streaks of indifferent color, and just stood there, letting the silence amplify the dull, aching emptiness inside.

[story 2]

"A B-minus," the professor had said, his tone utterly flat, devoid of any warmth. When I saw the final mark staring back at me, a strange numbness washed over me, quickly followed by a sharp, stinging awareness. My shoulders slumped, a sudden, heavy surrender to failure. I picked up my pen, but the graphite felt foreign, clumsy. My throat felt tight, as if a knot of grit was lodged there, making any attempt at speech feel impossible. I traced the lines of the incorrect answers with a fingertip, a gesture of profound dissatisfaction. I wanted to crumple the paper, to destroy the evidence of this disappointing outcome, but I couldn't bring myself to move, just sitting there, staring at the desk as if it were suddenly too much to bear.

[story 3]

I reread the feedback, the carefully chosen critiques sounding sharper and more damning than I had anticipated. A sinking feeling began in my stomach, a slow, cold tide rising up to meet me. My gaze drifted from the words to the stack of textbooks beside me, suddenly appearing alien and insurmountable. A slow, shaky breath escaped my lips, a shallow intake that didn't seem to reach my lungs fully. I started pacing the small space between my chair and the bookshelf, my movements jerky and undirected. Each step seemed to echo the hollowness spreading through me. When I stopped by the door, I leaned my forehead against the cool wood, closing my eyes tightly. The world outside seemed too bright, too loud, and all I could focus on was the profound sense of being utterly let down.
